narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've built a solid foundation with your undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average among students in our dataset, and you've chosen to pursue full-time studies like the vast majority of applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated your probability of failing at 44.3%—a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. This prediction was shaped most strongly by three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. Your undergraduate GPA had the biggest positive impact, pushing the model toward expecting you to pass by highlighting your strong college performance compared to peers. In contrast, your position in the lowest decile for Year 3 school grades was the second-most influential factor, nudging the prediction toward failure because it signals a concerning dip relative to the average fifth-decile standing. Your LSAT score of 29.50, well below the typical 36.70, came third in influence, also contributing toward the failure prediction by falling short of what the model associates with bar success. While your middle family income bracket aligns with many applicants, it didn't sway the decision much. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows strengths in undergraduate work but lags in recent school deciles and LSAT, which together tipped the balance. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many with similar backgrounds overcome these hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your Year 3 grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and reapplying next cycle with an updated profile that addresses these gaps. You might also explore targeted bar prep programs early to build confidence. With focused effort on these areas, your strong GPA foundation positions you well for a successful reapplication. Keep pushing—your determination will make the difference.
